read.title: Ghachar Ghochar
read.author: Vivek Shanbhag
read.review:
**spoiler alert** "oh its just a brief story on a poor family becoming rich, maybe it'll be a discussion on wealth distribution and greed but the power of family prevails etc etc"....or so i thought when i first read the synopsis. i was very happily surprised. yes, the family's dynamic is central to the story, but instead of the classic, heartwarming family story i was expecting, the family of ghachar ghochar felt much darker, complex, dynamic. it had the tidbits of everyday that made the family seem relatable, much like siblings, cousins, parents one may have in real life. but the underlying motivations and sentiment of each character was unique enough that it made the story vibrant and interesting, rather than a simple projection of family stereotypes on a story.

for such a short book, i also admired the deliberate pacing of the story. the story begins in media res, after the family has gotten rich, then backtracks to their prior situation, before jumping forward again. it was surprisingly unpredictable, lulling the reader into a sense that this is almost a typical "family gets rich suddenly" story, before drastically twisting into something darker towards the end. the conflict at the beginning, which seemed almost inconsequential (especially given the narrator's nonchalance), is in fact very consequential, and that realization only hit at the end.

there is a fair share of mysteries and unanswered questions throughout the story, but rather than seeming like messy loose ends, it only contributed to a very controlled, intentional, and growing sense of unease. i never understood how stories not adverstised as horror or thriller but instead of of "family life fiction" could make one uncomfortable, but ghachar ghochar did so beautifully, not with any explicit scares or messy drama, but with a quiet pulsing crescendo.

also man i hate the main character bruh like i know he was just a bystander but he reminded of of nick from the great gatsby but worse

read.title: Special Relativity and Classical Field Theory: The Theoretical Minimum
read.author: Leonard Susskind
read.review: first started reading this in high school so I could larp about knowing "cool" physics, then picked it up again in desperation and fear because I now actually have exams about it in college. in either case, all of susskind's theoretical minimum books excel at being accessible to a curious high schooler, but also in-depth enough to provide the foundation necessary for research and academics. i usually would not pick up a physics textbook to read for fun (sadly), but susskind remains an exception.

read.title: Cold Enough for Snow
read.author: Jessica Au
read.review:
wow. for such a short novel, it sure did take me a long time to finish. or really, it was because I read it in bits and pieces, reading on bus rides, during passing periods between classes, or on weekend mornings when I felt too lazy to get out of bed. I think I am glad for this. this is not a book meant to be rushed through in a sitting. it is meant to seep and trickle and slowly ooze.

the novel carried a quiet warmth that exuded in everything. and there was a constant distance, too, in the tokyo rains or the passing memories or in the vagueness of the unnamed daughter and mother's relationship to the very end. though seemingly formatted as a documentation of the pair's travels, at times we are transported into the daughter's recollections and emotions or the rushing flow of a nostalgic anecdote.

though none of these are fully described dramatically, we cannot help but feel its twinge regardless. descriptions are offered instead to the simple things: objects, art, a lake, and so many more. in this way, this story too is normalcy wrapped in a blanket and presented to the reader, asking, here. take this. this is my passing gift to you, and oh, won't you come inside and stay awhile?

there is a mystery that remains after the last page. but it'll just be that. here is a quiet, unassuming yet introspective tale that has passed through my life at this moment in time. it'll just be that.

read.title: One Day, Everyone Will Have Always Been Against This
read.author: Omar El Akkad
read.review:
i hesitated to rate this book—to even go on reading past a certain point—because of how little connection I felt. yes, I could nod in agreement, let a few tears escape out of sympathy, bring a hand up to my face in horror, but I couldn't get rid of the awful sense of superficiality it all felt to me. it is discomfort, to read so plainly, and in so much unfettered detail the reality that is so completely far from the coziness of my life. It is this discomfort that Akkad lays so clearly. it is this ignorance, when, spread as a blanket over the Western sphere and more, becomes permission for the worst of humanity.

still, you have to keep reading. and by the end, you too may have that pessimistic, pragmatic hope that one day this reality would at the very least be condemned by everyone, that there is no greed or pointing of fingers. until then, we keep wringing our hands and hold fast to these individual courages.

read.title: I Who Have Never Known Men
read.author: Jacqueline Harpman
read.review:
** spoiler alert ** it is without a doubt due to the curious, almost detached storytelling of the narrator that this book read so peacefully, even when describing tragedy. However, oblivious as the narrator may have been in the first half of the book, she did not lack sympathy—and it was endlessly interesting to me to watch her journey tying these knots of her understanding together, untying them again, and untangling them once more in her relentless search to learn to the very end.

on the other hand, it was this interest alone that drove me to continue turning the page. just as the plains were unchanging, there was rarely any excitement or thrill in the story. Still, I waited in anticipation to see if there would be that moment where the narrator Figures It Out, meets an ally, or simply escapes (but where to?).

as it turns out, she never truly does Figure It Out. She never does meet a man, much less another human being. And perhaps at the end, she really did find her own escape, but as the premise of the whole setting goes, she had simply traded her prison for a luxurious one. This plot, as a whole, is not fascinating in itself. It's not one that I expect to think about endlessly.

But I will think about it sometime, maybe, some boring day in the future when I am contemplating loneliness, or even taking this idea of living—of people—for granted. As the narrator hoped in her last days, her story did reach someone. Her story, which was so laced with quiet despair, yet unwavering hope—these feelings at least, will not leave.

read.title: Katabasis
read.author: R.F. Kuang
read.review:
i'm writing this much later than when i actually read it, but it is still on my dorm windowsill, and i still remember reading it when i had just entered uni, and the weather was still warm enough to read outside on the quad. i remember making excuses to read it all the time, bringing it in my bag everywhere "just to read one more chapter."

i have and always been a big fan of kuang's stories, and katabasis definitely did not disappoint me. from poppy war to babel to katabasis, i've always been impressed by the clear amount of research and world-building gone into each one of her books. coincidentally, i was also taking a logic/mathematical proofs class while reading, and i remember being so excited whenever seeing a reference to a theorem or paradox i had just done homework on. even for the mathematical (and even literary) references that i did not understand at first, i gladly spent time googling or reading more about it outside of the story--to me, the academic nature of the story's setting and characters was infectious, and i couldn't help but be curious about every facet of katabasis's magic system, the same questions alice and peter were asking, the thoughts behind each puzzle. maybe i'm just biased as a math person, maybe i'm just biased in general because i'm a kuang fan, but i was held in awe just as much by the writing as i was by the little nuances that kuang did not miss.

on another note, despite being centered around two academics, and set in the strict, cold setting of cutthroat academia, there were surprising moments of vulnerability that made the story shine. yes, it was rushed in some places, and was somewhat ambitious in the amount of backstory it attempted to convey (from the complex relationships between the characters to even academia politics stretching as far back to undergraduate years), but it was clear that each relationship was intentional, and constructed in a way that would be give purpose behind the characters' motivations. there was thrill, and shock, and a not-quite-cathartic-but-nonetheless-peaceful ending.

katabasis will always be a book i recommend to my friends. though it may have scared me (just a little) from pursuing graduate school. i'm sure one day i will pick it up again, perhaps a little more mature, perhaps a little more well-versed in all the academic references, and enjoy it again.

read.title: Invisible Cities
read.author: Italo Calvino
read.review: it bored me at first; it seemed just like an continuous stream of snippets of city descriptions. Of course the language of these descriptions were not simple--they flowed, and at times, were infused with various scents of melancholy or intrigue. but the real flavor of this book, the one that stuck with me long after and made me think of it whenever I catch the sight of moving figures and reflected buildings in the glass windows on campus, comes in the in-between conversations between Khan and Polo. were they ever real? maybe not, but perhaps neither were those cities. perhaps they have always been one and the same, and we only hold on to these wisps of imagined memory.

read.title: Bunny
read.author: Mona Awad
read.review:
oh what the hell. what a macabre, bizarre dark academia novel, hidden behind the classic posh-academic-institution setting. i don't usually read horror, but this book was surprisingly less on the pure scary side (possibly because the gore and general horror was written in such rich prose that it almost made if beautiful--or more so, just normal). if anything, it was more like a twisting labyrinth of words, taking advantage of the fever dream-like progression to evoke a general sense of unease in the reader.

like the setting of warren university, awad takes a really experimental approach to her writing, which at times really struck me as creative in her word choice or descriptions, but also just as often confused or disoriented me. fitting of a deemed 'horror' novel, her style definitely did fit in depicting samantha's debilitating mental state, and certainly never shied away from tearing apart the true horror of not murder or gore--but simple thought and imagination.

i will likely never read a novel like this ever again. reading this was like a stain on my brain that i'm still unsure will ever wash out. but it's here, and it's interesting in this strange, eerie way

read.title: Blue Sisters
read.author: Coco Mellors
read.review:
relationships are forever the most complex, frustrating things to exist, and sisterhood is no exception. mellors takes all the beautiful excruciation of it anyway, digesting it in the form of four complex individuals. each blue sister's story and arc are thoroughly traced--from their initial quirks and histories to their present struggles and beliefs. even nicky, whose death haunts the sisters' story from the first page, is not entirely absent. in fact, she is everywhere, and the trope of "haunting the narrative" could not be better described than with her.

the blue sisters and their story in itself is not revolutionary in that they are wild, one-of-a-kind-never-done-before characters. in fact, they are rather ordinary, and their struggles, painfully normal. it is in this normality that their conversations feel organic, their brokeness real, and their perpetual, anguishing hope solid. not one character is perfect. not avery, the oldest and the perfectionist. not chiti, the warm companion. not bonnie, the gentlest, and certainly not nicky, whom we almost always only see in a rose-colored lens, as she's painted as this sweet, happy sister whose circumstances were just unlucky.

the sisters' relationship winds together and apart, tangling, and untangling themselves again. their conversations are explosive, distant, and then warm nonetheless. this isn't a story that is extraordinary. but it is ordinary, and it is enough.

read.title: The Poppy War
read.author: R.F. Kuang
read.review:
**spoiler alert** "I have become something wonderful, she thought. I have become something terrible."

i'm writing this nearly a whole day after i finished reading and there's still so much on my mind. while it isn't unordinary for a book to have such a drastic shift from the beginning to the end, Rin's development--or really all the Sinegard characters' development--still shocked me nonetheless. in some ways, the rapidity of their story may be a flaw, but in the larger context of the raging war that pulled them from their innocent days at the academy, it was inevitable. as the reader, i was always hungry to watch it progress.

"Those weren't lives. They were numbers. They were a necessary subtraction."

it's clear that Kuang did her research, and part of the reading experience was also my amazement at every detail, whether it be references from Chinese folklore and ancient strategy or the thoroughly planned battle scenes. The Poppy War is influenced by East Asian history--and that includes the raw brutality and tragedy of it. it really is no understatement to say that Kuang did not shy away from the dark history of war. especially as the book spiraled to its end, it became clearer that there never was a "good guy" in this war to begin with, and this becomes a truth that Rin comes to turn with. even without the fantasy aspect, it was incredible watching how pure human madness, insanity, and desperation can unravel fragile morality. Rin is no exception. she is not a perfect hero, if she is a hero at all. the choices presented to her are messy, and we see her struggle to make a "right choice." we are not always rooting for her when she does make a choice. but as the reader, maybe that is ultimately who we are--spectators to this massive fictional war, saddened by the endless loss, horrified with every subsequent sacrifice, but maybe, just maybe, still holding on to that foolish hope that there will be some peace.

read.title: Alone With You in the Ether
read.author: Olivie Blake
read.review:
i'm writing this a few days after i first finished reading. i didn't plan on writing anything at all, but i really just kept thinking about this book. i really felt that i needed to immortalize something about this book.

 i didn't expect to enjoy reading a romance novel as much as i did. i didn't expect to love this book at all as much as i did.

by all means, i'm biased. i love math, physics, and logic--but i also love blossoming prose, anxious introspection, the "realness" of narratives that amaze me in my teenage naivete. this book satisfies all of these. can you love while you are broken? what does it mean to be fixed? can we even exist, just like this, in the mystical ether that has since been disproven by einstein's theories of relativity?

it was comforting, really, to read about regan and aldo's relationship, of the pure curiosity that blossomed into deeper intrigue into this sort of equilibrium-like state that was still all so fragile. contrary to the cliches and sappiness i expected from a dubbed "romance novel," i found myself faced instead with the raw worries and thoughts and complexities of the characters written in words so beautiful i found myself many times stopping and whispering the words under my breath again. i wrote them down, excerpts that made my heart feel. i paused other times, noting down the page number mentally so i can some day return and feel all over again.

i think in a way also it would not be complete exaggeration to say this book taught me courage somewhat. or maybe hope, more like? people are complicated creatures, and as aldo notes, they hopelessly cannot be contained in a single equation or formula. we're just here. we're trying.

maybe that's enough.

read.title: Piranesi
read.author: Susanna Clarke
read.review:
**spoiler alert** I thought for the longest time I would never finish this book. how could I, when the structure seemed so confusing, and piranesi’s narration (or journal entries) seemed so dull? but they were never dull. I found myself being just as curious about the world as piranesi, asking the same questions, fearing the same thing. it became comforting to read his narration, with his strange terms and odd structure that was nonetheless so logical. there’s a distinct warmth in the way the Halls became described. even through these pages, I feel it.

“The Beauty of the House is immeasurable; its Kindness infinite.”

it really is. it really is. i want to see it everywhere now
